content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

April 15, 1992

W.G. Wilson
Americans for Honest Government
2232 Hutchison
Vista, CA 92084

Re: Your Request for Informal
Assistance
Our File No. G-92-189

Dear Mr. Wilson:

This is in response to your letter regarding the campaign
disclosure provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").1

Section 82013(a) defines a "committee" as any person or
combination or persons who directly or indirectly receives
contributions totaling $1,000 or more in a calendar year.
Sections 82015 and Regulation 18215 (copy enclosed) define
"contribution."

If a citizens group receives or expends $1,000 or more for
political purposes during a calendar year, it qualifies as a
committee and must file a statement of organization (Form 410,
enclosed) with the Secretary of State, and file periodic reports
disclosing receipts and expenditures. The Commission has
determined that "political purposes" means supporting or opposing
state or local candidates, or ballot measures. (See information
Manual C on Campaign Disclosure Provisions of the Political Reform
Act for General Purpose Recipient Committees, enclosed.)

_______________________________
1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000, et seq. All references to regulations are to Title
2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

Wilson, W.G.
page 2


     If you have additional questions, please contact me at (916)
322-5662.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel
                                        [illegible]
                                        By: Wayne P. Imberi
                                           Political Reform Consultant

sections.question_synthetic: When does a citizens group qualify as a 'committee' under the Political Reform Act and what disclosure requirements apply?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: A citizens group that receives or expends $1,000 or more for political purposes (supporting or opposing candidates or ballot measures) in a calendar year qualifies as a committee and must file a statement of organization (Form 410) and periodic disclosure reports with the Secretary of State.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: No formal QUESTION or CONCLUSION sections found; content extracted from body of advice letter. Document provides statutory citations and references to regulations and forms. Minor OCR artifacts present but do not affect comprehension.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: When does a citizens group qualify as a 'committee' under the Political Reform Act and what disclosure requirements apply?

CONCLUSION: A citizens group that receives or expends $1,000 or more for political purposes (supporting or opposing candidates or ballot measures) in a calendar year qualifies as a committee and must file a statement of organization (Form 410) and periodic disclosure reports with the Secretary of State.

embedding.summary: FPPC response letter advising W.G. Wilson of Americans for Honest Government on the definition of a 'committee' under the Political Reform Act and the disclosure requirements triggered when a group receives or expends $1,000 or more for political purposes.
